name: The National
clientComment: For the sake of letting readers know we have collected the information, attribute quotes made to our reporters with told The National, but only on the first occasion and never in the first paragraph. It is not needed if the story carries an Exclusive label. Stories with first paragraphs such as At least 50 people died in a suicide bombing at a shopping centre in Damascus should not be followed by witnesses said, police said, doctors said or any variations or combinations thereof – especially not officials said. There’s time to introduce the sources in the second paragraph. And certainly not two police sources and local medics said. Equally, after Three major roads in Dubai will be closed for a month for repairs and maintenance, the added phrase the Roads and Transport Authority said is a statement of the obvious. It can also wait for the second paragraph. The medium through which the quotes are obtained is of interest to readers. Knuckles McGillicudy said in an email or in a phone call, or a tweet, or on Facebook. Readers must also know when we have obtained the information through other sources, which must be credited, but only after the first mention. If the agency is given a tagline to show the article was written by Agence France-Presse, we don’t have to attribute quotes to that agency. The National byline is provided when we have merged wire copy, with either others or our own, and quotes borrowed from agencies, even state-run agencies, must be attributed to them. However, if we are given different information from different agencies, as is often the case: Doctors told the Associated Press that 50 people died in the attack, but police quoted by Reuters said there were 51 dead, while Agence France-Presse said the death toll was 54, citing hospital staff. Prefer Reports of the number of dead ranged between 50 and 54.

name: The National
clientComment: People don’t speak in brackets, so there is rarely any need to use them in text. Use parentheses ( ) only for incidental information, such as currency conversions. Use square brackets [ ] only for explanatory text in a quote, to clarify meaning. When the words inside square brackets are removed, the sentence must still make sense. If it doesn’t, you have misused the brackets. Here’s how not to use brackets:“[Customers] have told me they won’t shop here at night,” Mr Al Shamsi said. “[The mall] is a hangout. Unfortunately, some parents think they can drop off kids who are 14, 15, even younger. This is not a babysitting service.” Use partial quotes or paraphrase instead: Customers “have told me they won’t shop here at night”, Mr Al Shamsi said. He said the mall had become a hangout for teenagers. “Unfortunately, some parents think they can drop off kids who are 14, 15, even younger. This is not a babysitting service.” We have had ridiculous uses of square brackets, such as “A[n] elephant …”  Just correct it and please, never use the sickly smug [sic]. It’s OK to make minor changes to correct quotes. If they require too much work they don’t deserve to be in the story, or they should be paraphrased.

name: The National
clientComment: Use King Charles III on first mention. After that, use King Charles, the king or the monarch. King Charles is fine in headlines. Don’t introduce him as simply the King or introduce Queen Elizabeth as the Queen because this isn’t a British publication. No caps in royal family. Jubilees, such as the platinum jubilee, are lower case. Use Prince William and Prince Harry on first mention. After that you can repeat Prince William/Harry or say the Prince of Wales or the Duke of Sussex. Don’t call them simply Charles, William or Harry. Please refer to their wives by their first names only on first mention when they are introduced as a couple (Prince William and his wife Kate, or Catherine in a very formal context). She could be the Princess of Wales on second mention. When they are introduced on their own, refer to them as Kate, Princess of Wales, or Meghan, Duchess of Sussex. Don’t use Markle or Middleton because these are their maiden names. It’s fine to call them Meghan or Kate in headlines as long as the context is clear. Use Queen Consort Camilla on first mention followed by the queen consort. Follow the same approach for other European or Asian royalty. But royal titles in the Middle East should be capped on all mentions whenever you are referring to the specific person (Saudi Crown Prince to visit UAE ).

clientComment: Like all punctuation, use sparingly and only for clarity of meaning, but take care to ensure their omission or inclusion does not alter the sense of a sentence. The England player, Wayne Rooney, has retired suggests that England have only one player. The England player Wayne Rooney ... is more accurate. Do not use commas in headlines instead of and. Use commas on four-digit numbers (1,000). Do not use an Oxford or serial comma before a conjunction (We appreciate good written English, sensible headlines and short captions). Its proponents contend that the serial comma is essential to avoid nonsense such as This book is dedicated to my parents, Winston Churchill and the Pope), but this can be achieved simply by changing the word order (...to Winston Churchill, my parents and the Pope). If you start a sub-clause with a comma, make sure you also end it with one. If you start a sub-clause without a comma, don’t end it with one. Don’t just use commas as a pause in a long sentence: they serve a specific purpose.

name: The National
clientComment: For US dollars use $ unless another type of dollar appears in the story, in which case use US$. If the context to the story allows, we can use A$ for Australian dollars, C$ for Canadian, NZ$ for New Zealand, and HK$ for Hong Kong. Use £ for the British pound, but spell out other references to pounds, including country to avoid confusion (40 Egyptian pounds, 600 Sudanese pounds). Use € for euro. For Japanese yen, we have the symbol in the glyphs pulldown. For £ and €, use the larger of each of the two symbols in glyphs. Spell out all other currencies (27 baht, 1,000 rupees). For dirhams, use Dh before the figure whether singular or plural (Dh500). Fils is singular and plural. Always use figures for currencies (6 billion Swiss francs). Spell out non-numeric references (the yen rose in light trading, the pound fell on new jobless figures). Spell out million, billion and trillion in all cases except when referring to currencies in headlines. Millions should be expressed to the first decimal point; billions to the second; and trillions to the third. We round down, not up. Beware of wire agencies that ignore foreign currencies and express all sums of money in US dollars, even in countries where the dollar is not in circulation. You may have to convert dollars to the local currency. Do not convert old transactions. It is meaningless.

name: The National
clientComment: January 1, 1999, but January 1999. Also, 1066AD, 200BC, although AD is rarely required. In print, use days of the week instead of dates for events within a week of the day of publication of an item. Periods of years should be written as 1992-1993 (not 1992-3), 2005-2009. Dates such as Fourth of July should not be made to conform to style.When working with body copy:Don't use today, yesterday or tomorrow online. Use, for example, "on Monday" to indicate something happened today.Don't use other days of the week in intros if it dates the story. For example, if a story is being published on Monday, don't have "on Sunday" in the intro. Move it down in the copy and change the intro to the present perfect tense ("has said"). If a date is less than a week ago or less than a week in the future, express it as "on Friday", for example, rather than "on May 13". But if there's a weekend between now and that date, you may need to say "next Sunday" or "last Friday" for clarity.If the date is more than seven days in the past or future, there’s no need for the day of the week. So write that the event will happen “on November 4”, not “on Friday, November 4” unless the fact it’s a Friday is particularly relevant.    Use "next month" and "last month" as opposed to in "in June" or "in April". Use last year and next year, not "in 2021" or "in 2023".  You'll have to break this rule sometimes to avoid repetition. Be careful with "next year" or "last year" when subbing in December or January.Use full dates only if really relevant or for effect in a feature. In most cases you can say, for example, "the city was captured in January 2009".   Single captions with stories:The rules above all apply. Try not to include dates or days of the week unless it's a today picture ("on Monday") or you need to make clear the events in the photo aren't new. If so, you can usually put, for example, "protesters march on the presidential palace in January".Don't use full dates except, for example, if it's a famous historical event. You might put "Pupils weep after the Columbine High School massacre on April 20, 1999".

name: The National
clientComment: Single fractions should be spelt out (three and a half) without hyphens. Always use the metric system, except when it is a firmly established convention not to and in sayings that are part of the language (a 850-square-foot flat; he was beaten to within an inch of his life). We do not use “m” as a shortener for metres, even in headlines, except where it’s relevant and cannot be mistaken for millions in Sport. As a rule of thumb, abbreviations for weights and measures are flush against the figure. Centimetres, kilometres, kilometres per hour, grams and kilograms are abbreviated to 1cm, 2km, 3kph, 4g and 5kg. Write them out only if talking in general terms. For example, the house was several kilometres away.  Use abbreviations for the following major measures only on second reference: • bpd for barrels per day (200m bpd) • Always write out the square in square metres, square kilometres and square feet. A special note about property stories: square feet remains acceptable when referring to single apartments and other small residential areas (the flat is 800 square feet). For larger areas, such as shopping malls, use square metres. Even larger projects should be referred to in hectares, which on second use can be shortened to a flush ha. Do not use any abbreviations if the measure is not repeated in the story.

However, there are so many exceptions (the carpet was less than 5 metres wide), some going back centuries, that the rule is really just personal taste expressed as an edict by pedants. clientComment: Compound words increasingly lose their hyphens as they are accepted as normal use, and reference to a recently edited dictionary is often necessary. The guiding principle, as with all punctuation marks, is that hyphens should be used only when essential for clarity of meaning. With most prefixes and suffixes the compound is written as a single word, but ex, neo, non, pro and self usually need hyphens, but note selfsame and unselfconscious. Co meaning fellow, as in co-driver, takes the hyphen.  Hyphens are also used to mark the difference between similar words (reform, re-form; resigned, re-signed), to separate identical vowels (pre-empt), before stems beginning with a capital letter (pro-British) and with like when it follows words ending in l (eel-like). The suffix less needs the hyphen after stems ending with ll (hill-less). Hyphens are used in compound adjectives formed from a noun and a participle (cloud-filled sky) or from an adverb and a verb when the adverb may be mistaken for an adjective (ill-gotten gains). In general, do not use the hyphen after adverbs (newly married couple). Note that a man earns Dh17,000 a month and so has a Dh17,000-a-month job. Hyphens are to be avoided in sporting terms: wicketkeeper, scrum half, etc.  Use hyphens with care to avoid confusion or unwanted hilarity.  Note motorcycle, motorcyclist, machinegun, sub-machinegun.  Avoid dangling hyphens (his two- and four- year-old children). Fractions are not hyphenated (two thirds of all acrobats, three quarters); except when adjectival (a two-thirds majority).  No hyphens in Latin phrases (in vitro fertilisation, postmortem examination).

name: The National
clientComment: It is an immutable law of journalism that all numbers in a story – especially phone numbers and bank account numbers – will be wrong. So check them. And check them again. Then check them again. If several figures in a story come to a total, is it correct? Check. Then, if there is time, have someone else check too.In general, write numbers one to nine in full in text, and 10 and above as figures. Same for ordinal numbers (first, second, third, up to ninth, then 10th, 28th, 100th, 133th, etc.The main exceptions are: • dates and times (The concert will take place on October 6, at 3pm). • addresses (12 Batt Lane) • figures in headlines, charts and tables. • decimals and percentages (Aldar stock was up 2 per cent on the week). • sums of money (A falafel costs Dh9).   For ranges in the millions and billions, repeat the word (The project will cost Dh5 billion to Dh7billion).In general, we round down figures in the millions to one decimal place (Dh4.7 million), billions to two decimal places (Dh5.43 billion), and trillions to three decimal places (Dh7.994 trillion).When rounding figures down, the figures $1.51 million to $1.59 million, for example, would be expressed as $1.5 million.Be wary of using rounded figures to calculate percentage increases and decreases. If the numbers in a story or especially graphics don’t add up because they were rounded, we need to explain why.

name: The National
clientComment: Do not use an apostrophe to indicate a plural (the Joneses, not the Jones’). Proper names ending in -y do not end -ies in the plural (the Marys, Trilbys, lay-bys, zlotys).  -is in words from Greek and Latin becomes -es: analyses, axes, oases. Words ending in -o take -s (not - es) except: goes, heroes, potatoes, salvoes, tomatoes, tornadoes (but Tornados for the aircraft), torpedoes, vetoes, volcanoes.  Virtuosos, solos, sopranos, but normally -o words of Italian origins used mainly in the arts take -i in the plural. -um in words from Latin becomes -a except for: referendums, compendiums, craniums, emporiums, mediums (but media as in mass media) memorandums, rostrums, stadiums. -us in words from Latin becomes -i only in: bacilli, bronchi, cacti, calculi, foci (only in certain rare scientific terms), fungi, gladioli, loci, narcissi, nuclei, radii, stimuli, termini. Opus becomes opuses and genius becomes geniuses etc. Do not use an apostrophe to form the plural of a set of initials (MPs not MP’s).

name: The National
clientComment: Usually confined to the Business section. Purchasing managers’ index on first reference. A monthly business sentiment tracker. The survey is conducted by Markit for the UAE, Saudi Arabia and Egypt and released by Emirates NBD. The PMI provides an advance indication of what is happening in the private sector economy by tracking variables such as output, new orders, stock levels, employment and prices across the manufacturing, construction, retail and service sectors. A score above 50 indicates the economy is expanding, below 50 is an indication that it is contracting.

clientComment: Nouns ending in -s add ’s in the normal way (lass’s, Charles’s, St James’s Square).To form the plural possessive, add an apostrophe to the -s of the plural (the lasses’, The Joneses’ dog). Expressions such as 10 years’ jail and a pound’s worth have apostrophes. Apostrophes are needed in expressions in which words such as shop or house are understood (going to the grocer’s, going to Mr Brown’s, but taking to the cleaners). This rule holds good for proper names (shopping at Selfridge’s, at Marks & Spencer’s), but when a business uses a title in plural form (Debenhams, Barclays Bank) no apostrophe is needed. If two people share the same possessive, give the apostrophe to the second (Gilbert and Sullivan’s Yeomen of the Guard).

The nadir was reached one holiday season with an advertisement for pre-prepared turkey.

This is net profit minus the non-controlling or minority interests. By excluding the minority interests, an analyst is able to better understand what income shareholders have a claim on. If the minority interests were included, the net profit figure would be overstated.

name: The National
clientComment: Use only in Business. Elsewhere, do not write, for example, the development will be completed in the third quarter, but the development will be completed by September. In Business, in reference to earnings, the use of quarterly on its own is acceptable: Emaar’s quarterly earnings fall. Prefer specific reference to last, final, first, second, third or fourth quarter rather than quarter on its own.

name: The National
clientComment: Do not refer simply to the queen when referring to Queen Elizabeth II. The queen (lowercase) is acceptable on second mention for all queens. Do not use Her Majesty or other such titles.

name: The National
clientComment: Use double quotation marks except for quotes within quotes, headlines, standfirsts, captions and other display text, when single marks should be used. If a quotation is longer than two paragraphs (which should be rare: see quotes), start each paragraph with a quotation mark, but use only one end quotation mark at the end of all the quoted material. Do not use quotation marks for verbatim reproduction of text, such as a speech, or in Q&As. Attributions should go at the end of the first quote. The exception is when a second speaker is immediately, directly quoted after the first. Avoid using brackets to insert missing information in quotes – this technique is frequently abused. Never start a sentence this way “[The government] should adopt the new measures,” he said. Use a partial quote, or better yet paraphrase. The government “should adopt new measures”, he said; he said the government should adopt new measures. Do not use quotation marks around the titles of publications, films or works of art in headlines or captions.

name: The National
clientComment: Quotes have to earn their place in any story. Do not write down everything people say and simply regurgitate it. We are journalists, not stenographers. If someone says something surprising, pithy, graphic, witty or apposite, quote it. Otherwise paraphrase, especially if it is full of industry jargon. Be careful when quoting someone whose first language is not English. If there is a reasonable expectation that they would sound intelligent and coherent in their own language, they should sound the same in English. If they do not, paraphrase. Quotations given in Arabic or another language should be rendered into idiomatic English, but be careful to preserve the original meaning. Partial quotations are acceptable – and sometimes necessary – but, again, be careful to preserve the meaning of the speaker. And let’s not overdo them. Avoid putting single words in quotations (the president called the project a “success”.) Beware of the redundant quote, a favourite of wire agencies: Iraqi special forces have advanced to within 100 metres of the Old City in western Mosul and expect to recapture it from ISIL within 48 hours, a military commander said. “We have advanced to within 100 metres of the Old City in western Mosul and expect to recapture it from ISIL within 48 hours,” Gen Ahmed Al Sayegh said.It’s important to make clear who is speaking so the reader doesn’t have to work too hard.  He/she said should be used only when it follows a sentence that gives the speaker’s name or indicates who they are.So don’t write:Mr Cooper said it was important to attribute quotes properly. A study last year found more than half of readers typically get no further than the third paragraph of a news article. The most common reason for giving up was that the story was written in a confusing way. “This is a quote but it’s not clear who’s speaking,” he said (this should say Mr Cooper said).But it’s fine to write:  Mr Cooper said it was important to attribute quotes properly. He mentioned a study that found more than half of readers typically get no further than the third paragraph of a news article. “There’s no need to use my name here because it’s clear who I am from the previous sentence,” he said.Try to put the attribution at or near the start of a long run of quotes.So don’t write: “This is a quote. You don’t know who’s speaking and it may be a long time before you find out. In the meantime, you may get tired of trying to work it out and stop reading the story. This would be a shame,” said Mr Cooper. But it’s fine to write: “This is a quote,” said Mr Cooper. “You now know who’s speaking and can enjoy the quote without having to waste time trying to work it out. This is a useful construction if you want to avoid two consecutive sentences starting ‘he said’. It’s also useful if the speaker has a long name or title that would kill the quote if you put it at the start.”It’s also fine to write:Mr Cooper said: “This is a quote. You now know who’s speaking and can enjoy the quote without having to waste time trying to work it out. This construction gives complete clarity.”There’s no need to use “he/she said” twice in a run of quotes.So don’t write: “This is a quote,” said Mr Cooper. “You now know who’s speaking and can enjoy the quote without having to waste time trying to work it out. There’s no need for further attribution,” he said.But it’s fine to write: “This is a quote,” said Mr Cooper. “You now know who’s speaking and can enjoy the quote without having to waste time trying to work it out. There’s no need for further attribution.”

name: The National
clientComment: If you use a full quote, it should come at the start. ‘Here for Abu Dhabi’: Salman Khan welcomes IIFA awards, not Salman Khan welcomes IIFA awards: ‘Here for Abu Dhabi’If you need a particular word at the front of the headline for SEO, use a partial quote.  Salman Khan says he’s ‘here for Abu Dhabi’ at IFFA awards openingIn this situation, you can always use full quote at the start of the headline in the mobile heading, which is unaffected by SEO.If you use a construction such as Trump: You call that a knife? or My knife’s bigger than yours, says Trump you don’t need quote marks because it’s clearly attributed.   The first word after the colon should be lowercase.

(There will be a few exceptions.) Names of specific races are uppercase (Asian, Latino), but black and white are lowercase.

clientComment: A good word: short and to the point. Use it, while avoiding explained, added, declared, claimed, insisted, opined, recalled, reminisced, laughed, shrugged, quipped, tweeted, said on condition of anonymity, told a press conference, said in a statement, told The National, etc. In reported speech, said should be followed by the past tense (He said the company would proceed with the share offering). The exception is when the attribution comes at the end of the sentence. Said is no longer the verb that governs the sentence and the first verb would normally be in the present tense (The company will proceed with the share offering, he said, not The company would proceed with the share offering, he said). This is a good reason not to interrupt an indirect quote with the attribution. The sentence changes tense halfway through. The present tense – says – may be used for features and other light pieces.

name: The National
clientComment: Get the key words that readers might be searching for at the front of the headline. This does not have to be done using a colon. Dubai fire: blaze kills two people and destroys buildings is better expressed as Dubai fire kills two and destroys buildings. Avoid simply using the name of a country followed by a colon as that reads oddly and does not improve SEO. So Yemen: seven injured in car bombing would be better as Yemen car bomb injures seven. In some cases, front-loading the headline with a colon is the only way to get the key words at the start. In that case, please make sure you add a short, reader-friendly mobile headline. Sometimes we have to leave a person’s full name in the main headline for SEO reasons. For example, it might say Joe Biden rather than Biden. In this case, please make sure there’s a mobile headline that simply says Biden.

name: The National
clientComment: Identify your sources whenever possible. Use anonymous sources only when they can provide information in the public interest that is not available from any other source. Do not easily offer sources anonymity; press them to go on the record or to give some other identifying detail to give the reader a sense of why they should trust the information – and you. Do not use agency-speak such as said on condition of anonymity because he was not authorised to speak to the media, or any other reason. Credit readers with the intelligence to work out that if we do not give a source’s name, it’s not because we forgot to ask. Do not allow anonymity to be used as a shield for an attack on people, institutions or a policy. If in doubt, consult your editor. Avoid sources said at all costs and never write an informed source, a reliable source or a person familiar with the case: if they weren’t, you wouldn’t be quoting them. Be transparent when a source has a vested interest in the story (The executive from Big Company, which owns 15 per cent of the target of the hostile takeover bid, said the company would launch counter-measures.)

clientComment: That is used with restrictive (or defining) clauses and which with nonrestrictive (or non-defining) clauses. A good rule of thumb is that that is never preceded by a comma, and which is almost always preceded by a comma.

clientComment: Use numerals when giving clock times followed by am or pm, and use a full stop – not a colon – to separate the hours and minutes (5am, 4pm, 6.30am, 9.25pm). But avoid redundancies such as 4pm in the afternoon. There is no such time as 12am or 12pm; use noon and midnight instead. Always precede the day of the week with the word on (The meeting will take place at 4pm on Saturday). When reporting background information, avoid phrases such as several months ago and recently – say exactly when things took place (Last September; on February 12). For longer time sequences spell out each word (He took 20 hours, 12 minutes and six seconds to break the world falafel eating record). For sports statistics use this style: 4:25.45 (four hours, 25 minutes and 45 seconds).

clientComment: Avoid when referring to people with serious illnesses. Not only is the word overused, it conveys a sense of pity. Write he has cancer, not he is a cancer victim. The word should also be avoided in crime and court stories – describe people by their names, nationality, occupation, etc before resorting to the trite the victim.

name: The National
clientComment: The Emirates News Agency is a useful source of information on local events, particularly the activities of the country’s leaders. However, Wam often blurs fact, opinion and speculation; it also publishes unedited press releases from private and public companies, and government ministries, departments, agencies and other entities. For these reasons, unless an important story is late and/or urgent, do not use Wam as the only source: we should try to do our own reporting.

name: The National
clientComment: Uppercase when used as a proper noun when referring to a specific, declared conflict (the Vietnam War, the Iran-Iraq War), otherwise lowercase. Write First World War and Second World War, but not WWI or similar variations. The term the Great War to describe the First World War is not incorrect, but should be confined to feature writing. Where war is not declared, lowercase war or find another term, eg Falklands war or Falklands conflict. We should also refer to the first Iraq war (1991) and the second Iraq war (2003).
